evidence_basis_summary: DiscoveryBench full text shows humans manually derived 264 discovery tasks from 20+ published papers, filtered 785 Zenodo repositories down to 14 candidates with only 3 passing inclusion checks, demonstrating heavy human relevance adjudication over what counts as an admissible task.
notes: upgraded from low to high after reading C014_S1 pdf full text documenting manual paper/repo selection pipeline

evidence_basis_summary: DiscoveryBench explicitly requires scientific semantic reasoning, e.g., deciding which analysis is appropriate for the domain (spatial autocorrelation for plant invasion; econometric modeling) and shows agents fail badly on domain-specific workflows (spatial analysis 0%, ecological modeling 0%), confirming humans still police mechanism validity.
notes: upgraded from low to high; C014_S1 pdf gives direct evidence on mechanism/method choice as human-coded workflow

evidence_basis_summary: DiscoveryBench formalizes hypotheses as context/variables/relationship triples defined by the benchmark designers rather than by situated construct debates; the benchmark sidesteps disagreement over construct meaning so the role is effectively absent in the case frame.
notes: upgraded from low to medium; benchmark formalization evidence supports not applicable with reasonable confidence

evidence_basis_summary: DiscoveryBench deliberately narrows to tractable public datasets (NLS, GBIF, World Bank) and ResearchBench restricts to 2024 papers to avoid contamination; corpus pluralism is not a design concern of either benchmark.
notes: upgraded from low to medium; benchmark scope described directly in sources

evidence_basis_summary: DiscoveryBench describes extensive human protocol work (90 person-hours per replication, workflow length scoring, HMS evaluation protocol design) but the case is coded no because this protocol work is upstream benchmark construction rather than a live research role being tested; source supports the not_applicable coding with decent confidence.
notes: upgraded from low to medium; note tension - benchmark construction itself is heavy protocol work,  but role is coded at the task-performer level

evidence_basis_summary: Neither benchmark probes whether AI-derived hypotheses transport across populations or designs; DiscoveryBench's DB-REAL and DB-SYNTH split is a task-difficulty lever, not a transportability test.

Both benchmarks operate purely on pre-collected datasets and published papers with no fieldwork, interviews, or situated respondent interaction; DiscoveryBench explicitly brackets after physical experiments and data collection and studies only the data-analysis part.
notes: upgraded from low to high; source explicitly brackets field/interview stage

evidence_basis_summary: DiscoveryBench notes heavy human labor (up to 90 person-hours per dataset, 30% failure rate) and credits humans for manual derivation of workflows, showing labor allocation pressure even as the benchmark aspires to automation; authorship/answerability concerns are not directly addressed.
DiscoveryBench uses a fixed context/variable/relationship formalism imposed by benchmark designers rather than emerging from contested construct definitions; split-candidate role is not exercised.
Both benchmarks use single gold hypotheses per task with HMS scoring that rewards exact/broad relational match, leaving no room for subgroup disagreement or representational pluralism.
DiscoveryBench does not pressure-test whether hypotheses travel across populations; DB-SYNTH varies difficulty rather than transport.

DiscoveryBench is explicitly a calibration/benchmarking case: humans design HMS (ctxF1, varF1, relacc) scoring, gold-vs-predicted hypothesis matching, Reflexion oracle feedback, and task-difficulty levels 1-4; calibration-architect work is central and observable.
notes: upgraded from low to high and changed role_observable_in_case from no to yes, status from not_applicable to split_pressure; text strongly contradicts prior coding - benchmark is explicitly a calibration design

evidence_basis_summary: Benchmarks are dataset-only with no interviews, fieldwork, or respondent interaction; role cannot apply.

DiscoveryBench explicitly tests domain-knowledge dependency for archaeology tasks and finds performance jumps from 9.9% to 17.5% when archaeology domain hints are added, directly showing tacit/contextual knowledge remains a binding human contribution.
notes: upgraded from low to high and changed role_observable_in_case from no to partial, status from not_applicable to split_pressure; archaeology domain-knowledge experiment in C014_S1 pdf directly contradicts prior not_applicable coding

evidence_basis_summary: Benchmarks concern hypothesis discovery from pre-selected datasets, not title/abstract triage or early screening of studies; role does not clearly apply.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_basis_summary: DiscoveryBench builds ground-truth hypotheses from published papers and uses GPT-4 as automated evaluator, and ResearchBench reports strong LLM performance on inspiration retrieval with minimal human intervention; both illustrate routine coder work shifting from humans to auditable AI plus human review, particularly for correlation analysis (55%) and data selection (18%) categories.
notes: upgraded from low to high and changed role_observable_in_case from no to partial, status from not_applicable to shrinking; ResearchBench abstract and DiscoveryBench ground-truth framing both directly speak to this shrinking cluster

evidence_basis_summary: Both benchmarks target structured hypothesis statements and scored workflows, not sentence-level prose drafting or manuscript polishing; the role is not within the evaluated task scope.
